parentQuestion: The receptor for static equilibrium is the macula.

scaffolds.q: Equilibrium means balance — knowing which way is up and how you're moving.
scaffolds.explain: Yes! The vestibular system tracks head position and movement for balance.

scaffolds.q: 'Static' equilibrium refers to sensing head POSITION relative to gravity.
scaffolds.explain: Correct! Static = not moving. Sensing 'which way is down' even when still.

scaffolds.q: The vestibular system is located in the inner ear, near the cochlea.
scaffolds.explain: Yes! The semicircular canals, utricle, and saccule share space with the cochlea.

scaffolds.q: The utricle and saccule contain sensors for linear motion and gravity.
scaffolds.explain: Correct! These two organs detect linear acceleration and head tilt.

scaffolds.q: The macula is a sensory patch found in the utricle and saccule.
scaffolds.explain: Yes! Each has a macula — a region with hair cells and otoliths for gravity detection.

scaffolds.q: Otoliths (tiny calcium carbonate crystals) sit on top of hair cells in the macula.
scaffolds.explain: Correct! Otoliths add weight — gravity pulls them, bending hair cells.

scaffolds.q: When you tilt your head, gravity shifts the otoliths, bending hair cells.
scaffolds.explain: Yes! Hair cell bending creates signals telling the brain about head position.

scaffolds.q: The macula detects linear acceleration (starting/stopping) and gravity orientation.
scaffolds.explain: Correct! Both linear movement and static head position shift the weighted otoliths.

scaffolds.q: The maculae (in utricle and saccule) are the receptors for static equilibrium.
scaffolds.explain: Yes! Maculae detect head position and linear acceleration using gravity-weighted otoliths on hair cells.
